title: A Review of Meletis and Dürscheid (2022)
subtitle: Writing Systems and Their Use and the typological limit of signed language writing
summary: Meletis and Dürscheid are strong at general grapholinguistics but typologically incomplete for signed-language writing.
synopsis: Writing Systems and Their Use is one of the strongest recent comparative theories of writing. The book meaningfully advances grapholinguistics by rejecting crude speech-dependence, giving writing internal structure, and taking visual form and typological self-critique seriously. Its layer-level strength remains clear, while its full-path adequacy is limited once signed language writing and SignWriting are treated as real writing on their own terms.

slides.title: Major Theory Source
slides.summary: Writing Systems and Their Use is treated as a strong comparative theory of writing, not as a weak foil.
slides.speech: This review treats Writing Systems and Their Use as one of the strongest recent comparative theories of writing. Its value is real: it moves beyond weak speech-dependence, gives writing internal structure, and takes materiality and typology seriously.
slides.notes: Open with respect for the source.

image_prompts.concrete: Grapholinguistics review slide showing Meletis and Dürscheid as a major theory source for writing systems, materiality, autonomy, and typology
image_prompts.abstract: A broad writing theory framework meeting signed-language writing

slides.title: Where It Helps
slides.summary: The book provides useful ground for discussing autonomy, graphetics, materiality, and larger written units.
bullets: The typology is strong enough to reveal its limit
slides.speech: The book helps the SignWriting discussion by opening better ground on autonomy, graphetics, materiality, and the internal structure of writing. Its typology is strong enough that its own limit becomes visible.
slides.notes: This slide shows why the review belongs in the series.

image_prompts.concrete: Review slide showing autonomy, graphetics, materiality, and typology creating useful space for SignWriting discussion
image_prompts.abstract: A theory framework opening several useful doors for plane-based writing

slides.title: Where The Framework Strains
slides.summary: The framework still appears incomplete when signed language writing and SignWriting are treated as real writing on their own terms.
bullets: Spatiality is acknowledged but not primary
SignWriting is mentioned but not field-shaping
slides.speech: The framework begins to strain when signed-language writing is treated as real writing on its own terms. Familiar linguistic units remain privileged, spatiality is not typologically primary enough, and SignWriting is not allowed to reorganize the field's categories.

image_prompts.concrete: Critical theory slide showing familiar linguistic units and line-shaped typology straining under signed-language plane writing
image_prompts.abstract: A strong framework bending under a spatial writing pressure test

slides.title: Platform Relevance
slides.summary: The review connects general grapholinguistic theory to the platform's claims about plane-based writing, authored space, and kinematography.
slides.speech: The platform relevance is that this major theory source helps clarify exactly where SignWriting presses grapholinguistics: plane-based organization, authored space, and the possible need for a term like kinematography.
slides.notes: Close by linking to Writing and Representation.

image_prompts.concrete: Platform relevance slide connecting grapholinguistic theory to plane-based writing, authored space, kinematography, and signed-writing typology
image_prompts.abstract: General writing theory pressured by plane-based signed writing
